editorial.titleTr: Is the President's View of Real Estate and Financial Markets Too Optimistic?
summaryTr: President Lee Jae-myung emphasized stability in real estate and financial markets.
Seoul apartment prices and jeonse deposits rose together, revealing a gap with reality.
Concerns were raised about the President's optimistic outlook.
editorial.bodyTr: President Lee Jae-myung expressed his determination to stabilize the markets during a press conference marking his first year in office, citing achievements in real estate policy, the won-dollar exchange rate, and stock market conditions. editorial.titleTr: Editorial: Did They Try to Teach Elementary Students the Korean War as China's 'Resist America, Aid Korea'?
summaryTr: War Memorial Museum pushes to include Chinese perspective in elementary school Korean War program
Controversial attempt to compare and teach differing interpretations between Korea and China
Questions raised over historical education direction in National Defense and Patriotism Month program
editorial.bodyTr:
The War Memorial of Korea in Seoul's Yongsan district has created and presented a program comparing the Korean War from the perspectives of both Korea and China to mark June as the Month of Patriotic Service. The program, designed for elementary school students in fourth grade and above, interprets the Korean War differently depending on the perspective. This raises serious concerns about educational appropriateness.

The museum's attempt to present the Korean War through a Chinese lens risks distorting historical facts for young students. The Korean War was an act of aggression by North Korea, backed by China and the Soviet Union, against South Korea. Teaching elementary students to view this conflict as China's "aid against the United States" fundamentally misrepresents the nature of the war and its causes.

Such a curriculum approach is problematic for several reasons. First, it creates a false equivalence between the legitimate defense of South Korea and China's military intervention in support of North Korean aggression. Second, it could confuse young learners about the origins of the conflict and Korea's struggle for freedom and sovereignty. Third, educational institutions, particularly those with national significance like the War Memorial, have a responsibility to present historical facts accurately to younger generations.

The museum should reconsider this program and ensure that any educational materials clearly establish the historical facts: that North Korea initiated the war as an invasion, and that China's involvement was in support of that aggression, not a neutral "aid" effort. Young Korean students deserve education that truthfully conveys their nation's historical experience and sacrifice during the Korean War.

summaryTr: Korea National Oil Corporation selects BP for joint exploration of deep-sea gas fields in the East Sea.
South Korea secures management rights with a 51:49 stake ratio, commencing exploration in September.
International energy cooperation proceeds despite Democratic Party's claims of a 'diplomatic fiasco.'
editorials.bodyTr:
BP's Deep Sea Investment: What's the Democratic Party's Position on What They Called a "Grand Deception"?

Korea National Oil Corporation has notified British oil major BP as the preferred partner for joint exploration negotiations at the East Sea deep-water gas field, dubbed the "Blue Whale Project." Exploration is set to begin in September. With a stake ratio of 51 to 49, Korea will hold operational control.

editorials.bodyTr:
The Unreasonable Demand for Nationwide Revoting Hidden Behind the Party Leader's Remarks

The Democratic Party's floor leader Jang Dong-hyuk held a press conference on the 9th, claiming that the only fundamental solution to the ballot shortage crisis is a nationwide revote. The ballot shortage crisis is certainly an unprecedented situation. If the ballot shortage were significant enough to influence election results...

editorial.title: [사설] 비밀 유출 논란 자초한 국방장관 발언
editorial.body: 입력 종합 26면 안규백 국방부 장관이 지난달 31일(현지시간) 제23차 아시아 안보회의(샹그릴라 대화)가 열리고 있는 싱가포르 샹그릴라 호텔에서 취재진과 만나 현안에 대해 설명하고 있다. [연합뉴스] 전시작전통제권(전작권) 전환을 위한 한·미 양국의 평가 수준을 공개한 안규백 국방부 장관의 발언이 비밀 유출 논란에 휩싸였다. 안 장관은 지난달 31일 싱가포르 출장 중 “한·미 양국은 2020년에 전작권 전환 조건의 94%가 이미 충족됐다고 합의한 것을 비롯해 우리의 능력에 대해 충분히 설명했다”고 밝혔다. 이날 아시아안보회의(샹그릴라 대화)에 참석한 미국 의원들을 만나 한국군의 전작권 행사 능력을 설명하고 협조를 요청했다는 것이다. 한·미는 조건이 갖춰지면 전작권을 한국군에 이양하기로 하고, 구체적인 매뉴얼을 만들어 평가를 진행 중이다. 하지만 구체적인 평가 항목이나 이행 수준은 한·미 연합의 비밀로 간주하고 있다. 자칫 한·미 연합 전력 수준과 방어 태세가 북한 등에 공개될 것을 우려해서다. 그런데 국방부 수장이 나서 양국 군 당국이 공개를 꺼려 온 94%라는 구체적인 수치를 언급한 것이다. 논란이 일자 국방부는 “조속한 전작권 회복과 관련한 국민의 알권리 보장과 이해를 돕기 위해 개략적인 수치를 제시한 것”이라며 “구체적인 내용을 적시한 게 아니어서 한·미 연합 비밀에 해당하지 않는다”고 해명했다. 하지만 국방부는 지난해 전작권 전환 평가 결과에 대한 국회의 자료 제출 요구에 “(전작권과 관련한) 평가 항목 및 결과 등 구체적인 사항은 한·미 연합의 비밀로 답변이 제한된다”며 비공개 입장을 고수한 바 있다. 전작권 평가 추정이 가능한 자료를 공개하지 않겠다는 뜻이다. 군 당국이 지금까지 7년 넘게 국회에 관련 내용을 설명하지 않은 것은 사안의 민감성을 방증한다. 미국은 지난 3월 정동영 통일부 장관의 북한 구성시 우라늄 농축시설 존재 언급을 이유로 아직까지 북핵 동향 관련 정보 제공을 중단하고 있다. 한·미 동맹에 균열 징후가 이어지고 있는 만큼 책임 있는 당국자라면 단어 하나에도 신중을 기해 발언하는 것이 맞다. 한·미는 어제부터 1박2일 동안 한국군의 핵추진잠수함 보유와 우라늄 농축 등과 관련한 협의를 시작했다. 현 정부가 역점을 두고 있는 전작권 환수나 핵추진잠수함 보유 등은 결국 한·미 동맹의 튼튼한 신뢰가 기반이 돼야 순조롭게 진행될 수 있다는 점을 분명히 인식할 필요가 있다.
editorial.pullQuote: 책임 있는 당국자라면 단어 하나에도 신중을 기해 발언하는 것이 맞다.
summary: 국방장관이 전작권 전환 평가 수준 94%를 공개해 비밀 유출 논란 초래했다.
군 당국은 한·미 연합 비밀이라며 국회에 자료 제출을 거부해왔다.
한·미 동맹 신뢰가 기반되어야 전작권 환수 등이 순조롭게 진행된다.
editorial.stance: 정부는 민감한 안보 사안에서 일관성 있고 신중한 정보 관리로 한·미 신뢰를 지켜야 한다.
editorial.titleTr: Defense Minister's Remarks Invite Classified Information Leak Controversy
summaryTr: The defense minister disclosed a 94% readiness assessment level for the transition of wartime operational control, triggering controversy over secret information leaks.
Military authorities have refused to submit documents to the National Assembly, citing the information as a South Korea-U.S. alliance secret.
The smooth progress of regaining wartime operational control depends on building trust in the South Korea-U.S. alliance.
editorial.bodyTr:
Defense Minister An Kyou-paik has become embroiled in a controversy over leaked classified information after publicly disclosing the assessment level between South Korea and the United States regarding the transfer of operational control. During a business trip to Singapore last month, Minister An stated, "South Korea and the United States agreed that 94 percent of the conditions for the transfer of wartime operational control (OPCON) had already been met as of 2020, and we have sufficiently explained our capabilities." He reportedly met with U.S. lawmakers attending the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore to brief them on the South Korean military's ability to exercise operational control and to request cooperation.

Seoul and Washington have agreed to transfer operational control to the South Korean military once conditions are met, and are currently conducting assessments based on detailed manuals. However, the specific evaluation criteria and implementation levels are classified as a South Korea-U.S. joint military secret due to concerns that operational capability levels and defense postures could be exposed to North Korea and other adversaries.

Yet the head of the Defense Ministry publicly mentioned the specific figure of 94 percent, which military authorities on both sides have been reluctant to disclose. When controversy ensued, the Defense Ministry explained that it had presented only a rough figure to guarantee public awareness and understanding regarding the early recovery of operational control, and that it did not mention specific details and therefore did not constitute a breach of South Korea-U.S. joint military secrets. However, the ministry previously maintained a position of non-disclosure when rejecting the National Assembly's request for materials regarding last year's OPCON transfer assessment results, stating that responses were restricted as evaluation criteria and results constituted South Korea-U.S. joint military secrets. This amounts to refusing to disclose information from which OPCON assessments could be inferred.

The military authorities' refusal to explain related matters to the National Assembly for over seven years demonstrates the sensitivity of the issue. The United States has suspended the provision of information related to North Korean nuclear developments since March, citing Unification Minister Chung Dong-young's remarks about the existence of uranium enrichment facilities in a unified Korean peninsula. Given the continuing signs of fissures in the South Korea-U.S. alliance, responsible government officials must exercise caution in every word they speak. Starting yesterday, South Korea and the United States began one-and-a-half-day consultations regarding the South Korean military's acquisition of nuclear-powered submarines and uranium enrichment. It must be clearly recognized that priority initiatives of the current government—such as the recovery of operational control and the acquisition of nuclear-powered submarines—can only proceed smoothly when built on a strong foundation of trust in the South Korea-U.S. alliance.

leanScore.label: Lean conservative
leanScore.rationale: 국방장관의 비밀 유출을 비판하며 한·미 동맹의 신뢰와 안보 체계 유지를 강조하는 관료적·보수적 입장으로, 정부 신뢰성과 한·미 연합 기밀 유지를 핵심 프레임으로 삼음

editorial.title: Two economies
editorial.body: Korea’s markets rise on optimism while growth potential deteriorates Stock markets have a habit of telling two stories at once. One is about the present. The other is about the future. When the two diverge too far, volatility tends to follow. South Korea's recent market turbulence offers such a reminder. The Kospi's sharp plunge on Monday, followed by a dramatic rebound a day later, reflected more than shifting investor sentiment toward semiconductors. It exposed a growing gap between the economy’s short-term successes and its longer-term capacity to generate growth. On the surface, much appears encouraging. Semiconductor exports continue to benefit from the global artificial intelligence boom. The Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development recently raised its forecast for Korea's economic growth this year to 2.6 percent. The stock market has repeatedly tested record territory. Yet beneath these favorable headlines lies a less reassuring trend. The same OECD projects Korea's potential growth rate at 1.66 percent this year and 1.52 percent next year, falling to 1.46 percent by the fourth quarter of 2027. If realized, it would mark the lowest level since the organization began compiling the data. Potential growth is an unglamorous statistic, but it reveals the economy's underlying capacity for sustained, noninflationary growth. Unlike quarterly growth figures, it cannot be boosted indefinitely by a single export cycle or a temporary surge in demand. It reflects the economy's underlying productive capacity. That is why the contrast between stronger near-term growth and weaker long-term potential deserves attention. The semiconductor boom has become powerful enough to lift national output, but not powerful enough to reverse deeper structural trends. Korea’s aging population, a shrinking workforce, weak productivity gains and slower capital accumulation continue to weigh on the economy. The result is a country growing faster today while becoming less capable of sustaining growth tomorrow. The movement of capital offers another clue. Korean firms are increasingly shifting production and investment to North America and Southeast Asia. Such decisions are understandable but also raise a difficult question: If opportunities abroad appear more attractive, what does that imply about the domestic investment environment? The foreign exchange market is sending an equally disconcerting signal. The won has hovered around levels not seen since the global financial crisis despite a record current-account surplus during the first four months of the year. Conventional economic logic would suggest that such a surplus should support the currency. Instead, the won has remained under pressure. Part of the explanation lies beyond Korea’s control. The prolonged conflict between the United States and Iran has reinforced demand for dollar-denominated assets. Expectations of higher US interest rates have strengthened the greenback globally. Yet external factors alone do not explain why the won has appeared particularly vulnerable. Foreign portfolio outflows and sustained overseas investment by Korean firms suggest that investors are evaluating longer-term prospects as much as current export performance. This is why policymakers should resist viewing recent market movements as merely technical or temporary. Verbal interventions may calm currency markets for a time. Emergency measures can dampen volatility. But neither addresses the structural forces reflected in a declining potential growth rate. The challenge confronting the Lee Jae Myung administration is therefore larger than stabilizing the won or supporting equity prices. Markets ultimately reward economies that expand their productive capacity, attract investment and sustain confidence in their future. Korea still has world-class manufacturers, technological expertise and considerable financial resources. The question is whether these strengths can be translated into broader productivity gains across the economy and sustained growth in the years ahead. The stock market's wild swings this week may eventually be forgotten. The slower decline in the economy’s growth potential will not be. Ticker symbols flash by the second. Structural realities endure much longer. editorial.pullQuote: Korea's aging population, shrinking workforce, weak productivity gains continue to weigh on the economy.
summary: South Korea's stock market surges on semiconductor gains while underlying growth potential deteriorates to historic lows.
The won weakens despite record trade surplus, signaling investor concerns about long-term economic prospects.
Structural challenges like aging population and workforce decline threaten Korea's ability to sustain growth beyond current export cycles.
editorial.stance: Markets are pricing in Korea's long-term growth challenges; policymakers must address structural issues, not just market volatility.
summaryTr: 남한의 주식시장이 반도체 호황으로 급등하면서도 기저 성장 잠재력은 역사적 저점으로 악화되고 있다.
기록적인 무역흑자에도 불구하고 원화가 약세를 보이며 장기 경제 전망에 대한 투자자들의 우려를 드러내고 있다.
고령화와 노동력 감소 같은 구조적 문제들이 한국의 현재 수출 사이클을 넘어선 성장 지속 능력을 위협하고 있다.
editorial.bodyTr: 한국의 시장은 낙관론 위에서 상승하는 한편 성장 잠재력은 악화되고 있다 주식시장은 동시에 두 가지 이야기를 하는 습성이 있다. 하나는 현재에 관한 것이고, 다른 하나는 미래에 관한 것이다. 이 둘의 괴리가 너무 크면 변동성이 뒤따르는 경향이 있다. 한국의 최근 시장 혼란은 이러한 사실을 보여준다. 월요일 코스피의 급락과 하루 뒤의 극적인 반등은 반도체에 대한 투자자 심리 변화 이상의 무언가를 드러냈다. 경제의 단기적 성공과 장기적 성장 창출 능력 사이의 점점 커지는 괴리를 노출시킨 것이다. 표면적으로 보면 많은 부분이 긍정적으로 보인다. 반도체 수출은 글로벌 인공지능 붐의 혜택을 계속 받고 있다. 경제협력개발기구는 최근 올해 한국의 경제성장률 전망을 2.6퍼센트로 상향 조정했다. 주식시장은 사상 최고치를 거듭 경신했다. 그러나 이러한 호재 뉘앙스 아래에는 덜 안심스러운 추세가 자리 잡고 있다. 같은 경제협력개발기구는 한국의 잠재성장률을 올해 1.66퍼센트, 내년 1.52퍼센트로 전망하며 2027년 4분기에는 1.46퍼센트로 하락할 것으로 예측하고 있다. 이렇게 되면 해당 기구가 통계를 집계하기 시작한 이래 최저 수준이 될 것이다. 잠재성장률은 화려하지 않은 통계지만, 경제가 지속 가능한 비인플레이션 성장을 창출할 수 있는 근본적인 능력을 드러낸다. 분기별 성장률과 달리 단일 수출 사이클이나 일시적인 수요 급증으로 무한정 높아질 수 없다. 경제의 근본적인 생산 능력을 반영한다. 이것이 단기 성장이 강해지는 것과 장기 잠재력이 약해지는 것 사이의 대비가 주목할 가치가 있는 이유다. 반도체 붐은 국가 산출을 끌어올릴 정도로 충분히 강력해졌지만, 더 깊은 구조적 추세를 되돌릴 정도로는 강력하지 않다. 한국의 인구 고령화, 축소되는 인력, 약한 생산성 증가, 더딘 자본 축적은 계속 경제에 무게를 얹고 있다. 그 결과는 오늘날 더 빠르게 성장하면서 내일 성장을 지속할 능력은 떨어지는 나라가 되었다는 것이다. 자본의 이동이 또 다른 단서를 제공한다. 한국 기업들은 점차 생산과 투자를 북미와 동남아시아로 옮기고 있다. 이러한 결정은 이해할 수 있지만 어려운 질문을 야기한다. 해외의 기회가 더 매력적으로 보인다면, 그것은 국내 투자 환경에 대해 무엇을 시사하는가? 외환시장도 똑같이 불안한 신호를 보내고 있다. 원화는 올해 첫 4개월 동안 기록적인 경상 수지 흑자에도 불구하고 글로벌 금융위기 이후 보지 못한 수준 근처에서 맴돌고 있다. 통상적인 경제 논리라면 그러한 흑자가 통화를 뒷받침해야 한다. 하지만 원화는 계속 약세 압력을 받고 있다. 설명의 일부는 한국 통제 밖에 있다. 미국과 이란 간의 장기화된 갈등은 달러 표시 자산에 대한 수요를 강화했다. 더 높은 미국 금리에 대한 기대는 글로벌 차원에서 달러화를 강화했다. 그러나 외부 요인만으로는 원화가 특히 취약해 보인 이유를 설명할 수 없다. 해외 포트폴리오 유출과 한국 기업의 지속적인 해외 투자는 투자자들이 현재의 수출 실적만큼이나 장기 전망도 평가하고 있음을 시사한다. 이것이 정책입안자들이 최근 시장 움직임을 단순히 기술적이거나 일시적인 것으로 보는 것을 피해야 하는 이유다. 언어적 개입은 한동안 환율 시장을 진정시킬 수 있다. 긴급 조치는 변동성을 완화할 수 있다. 하지만 둘 다 잠재성장률 하락에 반영된 구조적 힘에 대응하지 못한다. 따라서 윤석열 정부가 직면한 과제는 원화 안정화나 주가 지지보다 훨씬 크다. 시장은 궁극적으로 생산 능력을 확대하고, 투자를 유치하며, 미래에 대한 신뢰를 지속하는 경제에 보상을 한다. 한국은 여전히 세계 수준의 제조업체, 기술 전문성, 상당한 재정 자원을 보유하고 있다. 문제는 이러한 강점이 전체 경제에 걸친 더 광범위한 생산성 증대와 앞으로의 지속적인 성장으로 전환될 수 있는가이다. 이번 주 주식시장의 거친 변동은 결국 잊혀질 수도 있다. 경제 성장 잠재력의 둔화된 감소는 그렇지 않을 것이다. 시세 기호는 초 단위로 지나간다. 구조적 현실은 훨씬 오래 지속된다.

editorials.title: Two economies
editorials.body: Korea’s markets rise on optimism while growth potential deteriorates Stock markets have a habit of telling two stories at once. One is about the present. The other is about the future. When the two diverge too far, volatility tends to follow. South Korea's recent market turbulence offers such a reminder. The Kospi's sharp plunge on Monday, followed by a dramatic rebound a day later, reflected more than shifting investor sentiment toward semiconductors. It exposed a growing gap between the economy’s short-term successes and its longer-term capacity to generate growth. On the surface, much appears encouraging. Semiconductor exports continue to benefit from the global artificial intelligence boom. The Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development recently raised its forecast for Korea's economic growth this year to 2.6 percent. The stock market has repeatedly tested record territory. Yet beneath these favorable headlines lies a less reassuring trend. The same OECD projects Korea's potential growth rate at 1.66 percent this year and 1.52 percent next year, falling to 1.46 percent by the fourth quarter of 2027. If realized, it would mark the lowest level since the organization began compiling the data. Potential growth is an unglamorous statistic, but it reveals the economy's underlying capacity for sustained, noninflationary growth. Unlike quarterly growth figures, it cannot be boosted indefinitely by a single export cycle or a temporary surge in demand. It reflects the economy's underlying productive capacity. That is why the contrast between stronger near-term growth and weaker long-term potential deserves attention. The semiconductor boom has become powerful enough to lift national output, but not powerful enough to reverse deeper structural trends. Korea’s aging population, a shrinking workforce, weak productivity gains and slower capital accumulation continue to weigh on the economy. The result is a country growing faster today while becoming less capable of sustaining growth tomorrow. The movement of capital offers another clue. Korean firms are increasingly shifting production and investment to North America and Southeast Asia. Such decisions are understandable but also raise a difficult question: If opportunities abroad appear more attractive, what does that imply about the domestic investment environment? The foreign exchange market is sending an equally disconcerting signal. The won has hovered around levels not seen since the global financial crisis despite a record current-account surplus during the first four months of the year. Conventional economic logic would suggest that such a surplus should support the currency. Instead, the won has remained under pressure. Part of the explanation lies beyond Korea’s control. The prolonged conflict between the United States and Iran has reinforced demand for dollar-denominated assets. Expectations of higher US interest rates have strengthened the greenback globally. Yet external factors alone do not explain why the won has appeared particularly vulnerable. Foreign portfolio outflows and sustained overseas investment by Korean firms suggest that investors are evaluating longer-term prospects as much as current export performance. This is why policymakers should resist viewing recent market movements as merely technical or temporary. Verbal interventions may calm currency markets for a time. Emergency measures can dampen volatility. But neither addresses the structural forces reflected in a declining potential growth rate. The challenge confronting the Lee Jae Myung administration is therefore larger than stabilizing the won or supporting equity prices. Markets ultimately reward economies that expand their productive capacity, attract investment and sustain confidence in their future. Korea still has world-class manufacturers, technological expertise and considerable financial resources. The question is whether these strengths can be translated into broader productivity gains across the economy and sustained growth in the years ahead. The stock market's wild swings this week may eventually be forgotten. The slower decline in the economy’s growth potential will not be. Ticker symbols flash by the second. Structural realities endure much longer. summary: South Korea's stock market surges on semiconductor gains while underlying growth potential deteriorates to historic lows.
The won weakens despite record trade surplus, signaling investor concerns about long-term economic prospects.
Structural challenges like aging population and workforce decline threaten Korea's ability to sustain growth beyond current export cycles.
editorials.stance: Markets are pricing in Korea's long-term growth challenges; policymakers must address structural issues, not just market volatility.

editorial.title: [사설] 870만 도급제 노동자 최저임금, 더 이상 외면 안 된다

editorial.titleTr: Editorial: The Minimum Wage for 8.7 Million Contract Workers Can No Longer Be Ignored
summaryTr: For the first time in 40 years, applying minimum wage standards to 8.7 million contract workers is under serious discussion.
Protecting the survival rights of workers in legal gray zones—including those in special employment, platform work, and freelancing—is urgent.
The Minimum Wage Council must establish inclusive criteria when determining next year's minimum wage.
editorial.bodyTr: Forty years after the enactment of the Minimum Wage Act and thirty-eight years since the implementation of the minimum wage system, discussions on applying the minimum wage to contract workers are now in full swing. This represents an effort to establish a basic safety net that can protect the survival rights of special employment, platform, and freelance workers who have fallen into legal blind spots. The Minimum Wage Council, which determines next year's minimum wage, is at a critical juncture where it must make a responsible decision.

editorial.titleTr: Samsung, Nix Semiconductor Plants in Honam Region...Must Not Undermine Core Competitiveness
summaryTr: Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are reviewing the construction of a semiconductor factory in Honam.
The possibility of this materializing is high, aligning with the government's plan to attract investment outside the capital region.
While increased local jobs are welcome, corporate competitiveness must not be undermined by political considerations.
editorial.bodyTr: Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are reportedly reviewing plans to build new semiconductor plants in the Honam region. While both companies are drawing a line by saying they know nothing of such plans, the possibility of realizing this is gaining weight in light of the government's initiative to promote investment outside the Seoul metropolitan area. An expansion of corporate investment in provincial areas would have many positive aspects for job creation and regional economic vitality. However, there is concern that when political logic becomes involved, it could undermine corporate competitiveness as well.

editorials.titleTr: Homeowners' Property Tax Hike Calls for Careful Consideration
summaryTr: President Lee Jae-myung has signaled a hike in property holding taxes and is pushing to prepare amendments to tax laws for July.
Concerns have been raised that the government's strengthened real estate taxation could affect not only speculators but also homeowners with a single residence.
The position is that raising holding taxes on primary homeowners requires a cautious approach.
editorials.bodyTr: President Lee Jae-myung signaled a comprehensive overhaul of the real estate tax system at a press conference marking his first year in office, stating that "Korea's property holding taxes are generally low." The government has begun a comprehensive restructuring of the overall real estate taxation system with a target of presenting revised tax legislation in July. However, there are concerns that the blade aimed at curbing speculation is showing signs of extending beyond multiple property owners to affect single homeowners with genuine housing needs. President Lee personally mentioned capital gains taxation of non-resident single homeowners..

editorials.titleTr: Jensen Huang: "Age of Mechanical Engineering Returns"...Golden Opportunity for Manufacturing Power Korea
summaryTr: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang has declared the advent of the 'age of mechanical engineering.'
AI is expanding beyond software into the physical world—robots, manufacturing, and energy.
A new opportunity for an AI revolution has opened for South Korea, a manufacturing powerhouse.
editorials.bodyTr: Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia at the pinnacle of the artificial intelligence revolution, delivered a weighty message during his visit to Seoul National University on the 8th. CEO Huang emphasized that "if the past 40 years were the era of software engineers, now we are entering the era of mechanical engineers again." AI is moving beyond the stage of remaining as algorithms on screens or in data centers and combining with the physical world through robots, autonomous vehicles, manufacturing, energy, and biotechnology.
